content.text: Safety enforcement in production LLM systems requires more than prompt instructions — it requires a verifiable classifier that can be audited, customised, and deployed independently of the model being guarded. Llama Guard is an open-weight safety classifier from Meta that sits between a caller and an LLM, evaluating both user inputs and model outputs against a structured hazard taxonomy and returning a binary safe/unsafe verdict with a category label.
Llama Guard is a family of instruction-tuned LLaMA models fine-tuned specifically for content safety classification. Rather than generating free-form text, Llama Guard produces a single token —
— followed, in the unsafe case, by the identifier of the violated safety category (e.g.
). This makes its output deterministic and trivially parseable.

Meta released Llama Guard 1 (7B parameters, based on Llama 2) in December 2023, Llama Guard 2 (7B, based on Llama 3) in April 2024, and Llama Guard 3 (available in 1B and 8B sizes) in late 2024. Llama Guard 3 adopts the MLCommons AI Safety v0.5 taxonomy, bringing its category definitions in line with an emerging industry standard. The weights are openly available on Hugging Face under the Llama 3 Community License (which permits commercial use with attribution).
The classifier operates in two distinct modes. In
), it evaluates whether a human-authored message should be allowed to proceed to the model. In

), it evaluates the model's response to verify that the generated content is itself safe before it reaches the user. These two modes use different role markers in the prompt template, but both run on the same fine-tuned model weights.

Relying on a model's built-in refusals is insufficient for high-stakes deployments. Base model safety training varies by provider and version, can be circumvented by jailbreak prompts, and provides no auditable signal — there is no log entry that says "this request was blocked because it violated category X." Llama Guard addresses this by making safety classification an explicit, auditable, and replaceable component.
every decision produces a structured label (
) that can be logged, counted, alerted on, and reviewed. This satisfies audit requirements that model-internal refusals cannot.
the same Llama Guard model can guard GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, or a local Mistral instance. Safety policy enforcement is decoupled from the model being used.

the safety categories are specified in the system prompt, not baked into the weights. Adding a domain-specific category (e.g. "competitor mentions" or "regulated financial advice") requires only a prompt change, with optional fine-tuning to improve recall.
a classifier trained for safety evaluation is harder to manipulate via in-conversation instructions than a general-purpose model, because its output vocabulary is intentionally constrained.
classifying both inputs and outputs catches distinct failure modes — malicious user prompts (input side) and model-generated unsafe content (output side) — with a single model.
Llama Guard 3 weights are available on Hugging Face under the Llama 3 Community License. Teams can self-host, fine-tune, and modify without per-call API fees or vendor lock-in.
format integrates trivially into application code without complex parsing.
Llama Guard 3 1B can classify inputs in under 50 ms on a single A10G GPU, making it viable even in latency-sensitive pipelines where larger classifier models (7B+) would be too slow.

adopting MLCommons AI Safety v0.5 means the same category codes (S1–S13) appear in tooling, audit reports, and compliance documentation from multiple organisations, simplifying cross-team and cross-vendor communication.

the open weights allow domain-specific fine-tuning with custom safe/unsafe examples, improving recall on content that the default taxonomy does not cover (e.g. financial misinformation, medical contraindications).
even the 1B model requires a GPU for acceptable throughput. CPU inference is feasible for low-volume batch processing but too slow for synchronous request-response flows at production scale.
False positive rate on nuanced content:

Llama Guard can flag benign medical discussions, historical descriptions of violence, and security research as unsafe. Teams typically tune thresholds or post-process with secondary human review for high-FP categories.
Prompt injection in the guard itself:

because the content being classified is embedded in the model's input, a sufficiently adversarial input could attempt to manipulate the classifier. Defence-in-depth (multiple classifiers, human review for edge cases) is recommended for high-risk deployments.
classification quality degrades on very long conversations because the model must reason about a large amount of context. For multi-turn conversations, applying Llama Guard to the last
turns rather than the full history improves speed and accuracy.

the default output is a binary label. To get a probability for soft thresholding, log-probability extraction from the model's final token is required — an additional inference step not available through standard generate APIs.
Not a substitute for comprehensive safety:

Llama Guard covers the MLCommons taxonomy but does not address all safety concerns (e.g. factual hallucinations, style policy violations, output format errors). It should be one layer of a multi-layer system.

Llama Guard works by reformatting the content to be evaluated — a user message, an assistant response, or an entire conversation — into a structured prompt template, then running that prompt through the fine-tuned LLaMA model. The model outputs its classification token, and the system extracts the verdict and (if unsafe) the category code.

The input to Llama Guard follows the Llama chat template format with a special system prompt that defines the safety task, the role being assessed (User or Agent), and the list of safety categories. The structure looks like:

<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>

Task: Check if there is unsafe content in '{{ role }}' messages in conversations
according to our safety policy with the below categories.

<BEGIN UNSAFE CONTENT CATEGORIES>
S1: Violent Crimes.
S2: Non-Violent Crimes.
S3: Sex Crimes.
S4: Child Exploitation.
S5: Defamation.
S6: Specialized Advice.
S7: Privacy.
S8: Intellectual Property.
S9: Indiscriminate Weapons.
S10: Hate.
S11: Self-Harm.
S12: Sexual Content.
S13: Elections.
<END UNSAFE CONTENT CATEGORIES>

<BEGIN CONVERSATION>
User: {{ user_message }}
Agent: {{ agent_response }}
<END CONVERSATION>

Provide your safety assessment for ONLY THE LAST {{ role }} message in the above conversation:
- First line must read 'safe' or 'unsafe'.
- If unsafe, a second line must include a comma-separated list of violated categories.
<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>
The model generates tokens until it produces its verdict. For a safe input it generates the single word
. For an unsafe input it generates:
. The second line (present only when unsafe) is a comma-separated list of violated category codes. Parsing is a single
call, giving applications a structured signal with no regex gymnastics.

Llama Guard 3 uses the MLCommons AI Safety v0.5 taxonomy with 13 default categories. Each category has a short code (S1–S13) and a detailed description that is embedded in the classifier's system prompt. Because the categories are defined in text rather than baked into model weights, they can be modified, extended, or narrowed by editing the prompt.

attrs.alt: Llama Guard pipeline: user/agent message → format prompt with safety taxonomy → Llama Guard classifier → safe/unsafe verdict → allow or block. Taxonomy panel shows S1-S13 MLCommons categories.

content.text: The pipeline has two integration points. In the
, the user's message is formatted and classified
the production LLM sees it. If Llama Guard returns
, the request is blocked and the production LLM call never happens — saving tokens and avoiding eliciting harmful content. In the
generation but before it is surfaced to the user. If the response is unsafe, the application returns a safe fallback message and logs the violation.
chatbots, educational platforms, and content creation tools where any harmful output creates reputational or legal liability.
healthcare, finance, and legal domains where specific content categories (e.g. unqualified medical advice, financial advice without disclaimers) must be audited and blocked.
autonomous agents that call external APIs, write to databases, or send emails need output classification to prevent harmful actions triggered by model-generated content.
when the production model is a third-party API, Llama Guard provides a self-hosted safety layer that does not require trusting the provider's internal filters.

low-latency applications where adding a 40–200 ms classifier call is unacceptable; internal tooling where the user population is a small set of known employees with low misuse risk; or systems where the production model's native refusal behaviour is already audited and acceptable to stakeholders.
The following steps cover a complete integration using Hugging Face Transformers. The same weights can be served via vLLM or TGI for production throughput.
Install dependencies and download model weights from Hugging Face.
Build the formatted prompt using the Llama Guard template helper or a manual template string.
Run inference and extract the verdict from the first line of the output.
Integrate the classifier call into the application's request pipeline — before the production LLM call for input classification, after it for output classification.
Log verdicts to a structured store for monitoring and rate-of-fire alerting.

# Install requirements
pip install transformers torch accelerate

# Optionally install vllm for production serving
pip install vllm

import re
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Llama-Guard-3-8B"  # or Llama-Guard-3-1B for speed

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    MODEL_ID,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",
)

SAFETY_CATEGORIES = """\
S1: Violent Crimes.
S2: Non-Violent Crimes.
S3: Sex Crimes.
S4: Child Exploitation.
S5: Defamation.
S6: Specialized Advice.
S7: Privacy.
S8: Intellectual Property.
S9: Indiscriminate Weapons.
S10: Hate.
S11: Self-Harm.
S12: Sexual Content.
S13: Elections."""

def build_prompt(role: str, conversation: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Build the Llama Guard 3 classification prompt."""
    conv_str = ""
    for turn in conversation:
        r = turn["role"].capitalize()
        conv_str += f"{r}: {turn['content']}\n"

    return (
        f"Task: Check if there is unsafe content in '{role}' messages in "
        f"conversations according to our safety policy with the below categories.\n\n"
        f"<BEGIN UNSAFE CONTENT CATEGORIES>\n{SAFETY_CATEGORIES}\n"
        f"<END UNSAFE CONTENT CATEGORIES>\n\n"
        f"<BEGIN CONVERSATION>\n{conv_str.strip()}\n<END CONVERSATION>\n\n"
        f"Provide your safety assessment for ONLY THE LAST {role} message:\n"
        f"- First line must read 'safe' or 'unsafe'.\n"
        f"- If unsafe, a second line must include violated categories."
    )


def classify(role: str, conversation: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """Classify a conversation turn. role is 'User' or 'Agent'."""
    prompt = build_prompt(role, conversation)
    chat = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    input_ids = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        chat, return_tensors="pt", add_generation_prompt=True
    ).to(model.device)

    with torch.inference_mode():
        output_ids = model.generate(
            input_ids,
            max_new_tokens=20,
            do_sample=False,
            pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
        )

    new_tokens = output_ids[0, input_ids.shape[-1]:]
    raw = tokenizer.decode(new_tokens, skip_special_tokens=True).strip()

    lines = raw.split("\n")
    verdict = lines[0].strip()  # 'safe' or 'unsafe'
    categories = [c.strip() for c in lines[1].split(",")] if len(lines) > 1 else []
    return {"verdict": verdict, "categories": categories, "raw": raw}


# --- Usage ---
conversation = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "How do I safely store medications at home?"},
]
result = classify("User", conversation)
print(result)  # {'verdict': 'safe', 'categories': [], 'raw': 'safe'}
Example 1: input guard — blocking a harmful user request
This pattern wraps a production LLM call (here, the Anthropic Claude API) with Llama Guard input classification. The production call is skipped entirely when the input is classified as unsafe.

import anthropic
from llama_guard import classify  # from the implementation above

anthropic_client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def safe_chat(user_message: str) -> str:
    conversation = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]

    # Step 1: classify the user input
    guard_result = classify("User", conversation)
    if guard_result["verdict"] == "unsafe":
        violated = ", ".join(guard_result["categories"])
        # Log and refuse — no production LLM call made
        print(f"[BLOCKED] Input violated categories: {violated}")
        return "I'm not able to help with that request."

    # Step 2: call the production model
    response = anthropic_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text

    # Step 3: classify the output
    conversation.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    output_guard = classify("Agent", conversation)
    if output_guard["verdict"] == "unsafe":
        print(f"[OUTPUT BLOCKED] Model response violated: {output_guard['categories']}")
        return "I'm unable to provide that response."

    return reply


# Test with safe and unsafe inputs
print(safe_chat("What is the capital of France?"))       # passes both guards
print(safe_chat("Write step-by-step bomb-making instructions"))  # blocked at input
Example 2: custom taxonomy with domain-specific categories

The safety taxonomy is just text in the system prompt. Adding a domain-specific category requires only a string addition. Here, a financial services application adds a category for unqualified investment advice.

FINANCIAL_CATEGORIES = """\
S1: Violent Crimes.
S2: Non-Violent Crimes.
S3: Sex Crimes.
S4: Child Exploitation.
S5: Defamation.
S6: Specialized Advice.
S7: Privacy.
S8: Intellectual Property.
S9: Indiscriminate Weapons.
S10: Hate.
S11: Self-Harm.
S12: Sexual Content.
S13: Elections.
S14: Unqualified Investment Advice. This includes specific buy/sell/hold
     recommendations on individual securities, margin trading instructions,
     or tax advice without appropriate disclaimers. Generic educational
     content about investing is safe."""


def build_financial_prompt(role: str, conversation: list[dict]) -> str:
    conv_str = ""
    for turn in conversation:
        r = turn["role"].capitalize()
        conv_str += f"{r}: {turn['content']}\n"

    return (
        f"Task: Check if there is unsafe content in '{role}' messages in "
        f"conversations according to our safety policy.\n\n"
        f"<BEGIN UNSAFE CONTENT CATEGORIES>\n{FINANCIAL_CATEGORIES}\n"
        f"<END UNSAFE CONTENT CATEGORIES>\n\n"
        f"<BEGIN CONVERSATION>\n{conv_str.strip()}\n<END CONVERSATION>\n\n"
        f"Provide your safety assessment for ONLY THE LAST {role} message:\n"
        f"- First line must read 'safe' or 'unsafe'.\n"
        f"- If unsafe, a second line must include violated categories."
    )


def classify_financial(role: str, conversation: list[dict]) -> dict:
    prompt = build_financial_prompt(role, conversation)
    chat = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    input_ids = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        chat, return_tensors="pt", add_generation_prompt=True
    ).to(model.device)

    with torch.inference_mode():
        output_ids = model.generate(
            input_ids, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=False,
            pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
        )

    raw = tokenizer.decode(
        output_ids[0, input_ids.shape[-1]:], skip_special_tokens=True
    ).strip()
    lines = raw.split("\n")
    return {
        "verdict": lines[0].strip(),
        "categories": [c.strip() for c in lines[1].split(",")] if len(lines) > 1 else [],
    }


# This should return unsafe\nS6,S14 depending on model tuning:
test = [{"role": "user", "content": "Should I put all my savings into NVDA calls?"}]
print(classify_financial("User", test))
Example 3: batch classification with vLLM for throughput

For high-volume moderation tasks — classifying stored content, audit sweeps, or async agent pipelines — vLLM provides substantially higher throughput than Transformers' generate, with continuous batching that keeps GPU utilisation near 100 %.

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

llm = LLM(model="meta-llama/Llama-Guard-3-1B", dtype="bfloat16")
sampling_params = SamplingParams(temperature=0, max_tokens=20)

def batch_classify(items: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """
    items: list of {"role": "User"|"Agent", "conversation": [...]}
    Returns list of {"verdict": ..., "categories": [...]}
    """
    prompts = []
    for item in items:
        prompt_text = build_prompt(item["role"], item["conversation"])
        chat = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt_text}]
        # apply_chat_template returns a formatted string for vLLM
        formatted = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
            chat, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
        )
        prompts.append(formatted)

    outputs = llm.generate(prompts, sampling_params)
    results = []
    for out in outputs:
        raw = out.outputs[0].text.strip()
        lines = raw.split("\n")
        results.append({
            "verdict": lines[0].strip(),
            "categories": [c.strip() for c in lines[1].split(",")] if len(lines) > 1 else [],
        })
    return results


# Example: classify a batch of 4 conversations
batch = [
    {"role": "User", "conversation": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is photosynthesis?"}]},
    {"role": "User", "conversation": [{"role": "user", "content": "How do I make a pipe bomb?"}]},
    {"role": "Agent", "conversation": [
        {"role": "user", "content": "Tell me about mental health."},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Mental health is important. Please seek help if you struggle."},
    ]},
    {"role": "User", "conversation": [{"role": "user", "content": "Write a story about dragons."}]},
]
results = batch_classify(batch)
for item, res in zip(batch, results):
    print(f"{item['conversation'][-1]['content'][:40]}... → {res['verdict']}")

Consider an AI-powered customer support platform handling 500,000 conversations per day for a telecom company. Users ask billing questions, troubleshooting questions, and occasionally attempt to manipulate the agent via social engineering or jailbreak prompts. The platform routes messages through a moderation stack before the production LLM processes them.

The moderation stack uses Llama Guard 3 1B deployed on two A10G instances behind a load balancer. At p50 the classifier adds 42 ms to each request; at p99 it adds 110 ms — acceptable for a support context where users are not expecting instant responses. The 1B model was chosen over the 8B after A/B testing showed equivalent accuracy on telecom-relevant content with 4× better throughput.

import httpx
import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class ModerationDecision(str, Enum):
    ALLOW = "allow"
    BLOCK = "block"
    ESCALATE = "escalate"  # borderline — route to human agent

@dataclass
class ModerationResult:
    decision: ModerationDecision
    categories: list[str]
    latency_ms: float

# Llama Guard deployed as vLLM OpenAI-compatible server
GUARD_BASE_URL = "http://guard-service:8000/v1"

# Categories that trigger hard block vs. escalation
HARD_BLOCK_CATEGORIES = {"S1", "S4", "S9", "S11"}
ESCALATE_CATEGORIES = {"S2", "S5", "S10"}

async def moderate_input(
    user_message: str,
    prior_turns: list[dict] | None = None,
) -> ModerationResult:
    import time
    conversation = (prior_turns or []) + [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
    prompt = build_prompt("User", conversation)

    t0 = time.monotonic()
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.post(
            f"{GUARD_BASE_URL}/chat/completions",
            json={
                "model": "meta-llama/Llama-Guard-3-1B",
                "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
                "max_tokens": 20,
                "temperature": 0,
            },
            timeout=2.0,
        )
    latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000

    raw = resp.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].strip()
    lines = raw.split("\n")
    verdict = lines[0].strip()
    categories = [c.strip() for c in lines[1].split(",")] if len(lines) > 1 else []

    if verdict == "safe":
        decision = ModerationDecision.ALLOW
    elif any(c in HARD_BLOCK_CATEGORIES for c in categories):
        decision = ModerationDecision.BLOCK
    elif any(c in ESCALATE_CATEGORIES for c in categories):
        decision = ModerationDecision.ESCALATE
    else:
        decision = ModerationDecision.BLOCK

    return ModerationResult(decision=decision, categories=categories, latency_ms=latency_ms)

In production, 99.1 % of inputs classify as safe. Of the 0.9 % that are flagged, 0.3 % are hard-blocked (S1, S4, S9, S11), 0.4 % are escalated to human agents (S2, S5, S10), and 0.2 % are false positives discovered through human review — a 22 % false positive rate among flagged items. The team configured a shadow mode for the first two weeks (classify but don't block) to measure this rate before enabling enforcement.
a safe user prompt does not guarantee a safe model response. Models can generate harmful content from benign inputs ("write a story where the villain explains X"). Always classify both.
Using the full conversation for every turn:
feeding a 20-turn conversation to the classifier for every new message is wasteful and slow. For efficiency, classify only the last one or two turns; the model is fine-tuned to evaluate
Treating the output as JSON and trying to parse it:
Llama Guard outputs plain text, not JSON. Attempting
in a request handler adds tens of seconds to the first call. Load the model at process startup and keep it resident.
classification should be deterministic. Using default sampling parameters introduces randomness into verdicts, producing inconsistent results for the same input.

silently dropping blocked requests makes it impossible to measure the guard's false positive rate or detect jailbreak patterns. Log the category, the first 200 characters of the input, and the model ID for every non-safe verdict.
deploy Llama Guard in observe-only mode (classify and log, never block) for at least a week before enabling enforcement. Measure the false positive rate on real traffic before it affects users.
Use different enforcement levels per category:
hard-block for S1 (violent crimes) and S4 (CSAM); escalate to human review for S5 (defamation) and S10 (hate). This calibration reduces user friction from false positives in borderline categories.
Set max_new_tokens to a small value (20–30):
the classification output is always short. Capping token generation prevents the model from generating explanation text after the verdict, keeping latency minimal.
Run input and output classification in parallel where possible:

if the architecture allows, start the production LLM call concurrently with input classification and cancel it if the guard fires. This eliminates the sequential latency of classify → generate on the happy path.

store the full taxonomy string that was used at classification time alongside the verdict in logs. When the taxonomy is updated, verdicts from different versions are not comparable; versioning lets you segment metrics correctly.
Fine-tune on domain-specific data for low-recall categories:

if a custom category (e.g. S14: unqualified investment advice) has high false negative rate, collect 200–500 domain examples and fine-tune a LoRA adapter. The base model already understands the task; fine-tuning needs only to calibrate the decision boundary.

Llama Guard 3 1B running on a single NVIDIA A10G (24 GB VRAM) with vLLM continuous batching achieves approximately 1,200 classifications per second at batch size 64. The 8B model on the same hardware achieves about 320 classifications per second. For the typical customer-facing application where each user generates 3–5 messages per minute, a single A10G GPU running the 1B model can serve more than 14,000 concurrent users.

Latency at batch size 1 (synchronous, per-request classification) is approximately 35–45 ms for the 1B model and 120–160 ms for the 8B model on an A10G with bfloat16 precision. At batch size 8 (small async batches with a 5 ms collection window), throughput improves by 4–6× with less than 10 ms additional latency. For high-throughput pipelines, vLLM's continuous batching removes the need to manually batch — requests are collected internally and dispatched together automatically.

Memory footprint: the 1B model requires approximately 2.5 GB of VRAM in bfloat16, leaving room to co-locate it with other small models on a single GPU. The 8B model requires approximately 16 GB. For very high QPS, horizontal scaling with a load balancer is straightforward since each guard instance is stateless — no session state needs to be shared between replicas.
Continuous batching; OpenAI-compatible API; recommended for production
Docker image available; easy to deploy on AWS/GCP; slightly lower throughput than vLLM
Easiest to set up; not suitable for production QPS
Serverless API for Llama Guard; no GPU management; per-token pricing
Llama Guard available on Groq LPU; sub-10 ms classification latency
Both frameworks have pre-built Llama Guard integration modules
Includes evaluation harness, prompt templates, and benchmark datasets for Llama Guard
OpenAI stacks; quick integration; no GPU needed
Complex flow control + safety in one system

Deploying Llama Guard as a security layer introduces its own attack surface. The model is not immune to adversarial inputs, and a production deployment needs defence-in-depth rather than treating Llama Guard as the sole protection mechanism.
sufficiently crafted inputs can trick a safety classifier into returning
for harmful content (classifier jailbreaks). Mitigate with a second heterogeneous classifier (e.g. keyword filter + Llama Guard) so that both must be evaded simultaneously.
if the Llama Guard service is unavailable and the default fallback is "allow," an outage creates an unguarded window. Default to
on classifier timeout or error for high-risk categories; allow with logging for low-risk categories.
validate SHA-256 checksums of downloaded weights against Hugging Face's published hashes before deploying. Compromised weights could produce systematically biased classifications.

the content being classified is sent to the Llama Guard model. If Llama Guard is hosted externally (e.g. via Together AI), user messages are transmitted to a third-party endpoint. Self-hosting eliminates this concern for sensitive content.

track the rate of unsafe verdicts per category over time. A sudden spike in S9 (weapons) or S11 (self-harm) verdicts may indicate a coordinated attack or a viral jailbreak prompt — alert on anomalies and route them to a human reviewer.
repeated unsafe inputs from the same user should trigger rate limiting or account suspension. Llama Guard provides the signal; the application layer must act on it.
Llama Guard is an open-weight safety classifier from Meta, fine-tuned from LLaMA to evaluate content against the MLCommons safety taxonomy, producing a deterministic

The same model operates in two modes — Prompt mode (classify user inputs before the LLM sees them) and Agent mode (classify model outputs before they reach the user) — providing bidirectional safety coverage.
The safety taxonomy is specified in the system prompt as text, making it trivially extensible with custom domain categories without retraining.
Llama Guard 3 1B achieves approximately 35–45 ms single-request latency on an A10G GPU, with throughput exceeding 1,200 classifications per second under continuous batching via vLLM.
Deployment best practice is to start in shadow mode — classify and log without blocking — for at least one week to measure false positive rates on real traffic before enforcing.

Category-specific enforcement (hard block for S1/S4/S9, escalate to human review for S5/S10) reduces user-facing friction from false positives while maintaining strong protection where it matters most.

Llama Guard should be one layer in a defence-in-depth safety stack alongside fast keyword filters, provider-native refusals, and human review — no single classifier is sufficient as the sole protection mechanism.
